title: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.title: Understanding Chagas Disease Transmission Dynamics
items.content: Chagas disease, caused by T. cruzi, is primarily transmitted through vectorial means via triatomine bugs, but can also occur nonvectorially.
keyPoints: T. cruzi causes Chagas disease, a zoonosis with significant chronic complications.
Vector transmission occurs when infective metacyclic forms enter the body through skin breaks or mucosae during a triatomine bite.
Nonvectorial modes include congenital (1–10% of newborns), blood transfusion (1.7% overall risk, 13% for platelet recipients), and oral transmission via contaminated food or drink.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.title: Clinical Impact of Chagas Disease Transmission Pathways
items.content: The spread of Chagas disease is influenced by both vectorial and nonvectorial transmission, affecting clinical management.
keyPoints: Chagas disease has a significant human burden in Latin America and globally due to its asymptomatic nature and chronic complications.
Congenital transmission occurs in 1–10% of newborns from infected mothers.
Risk of infection through blood transfusion varies: overall 1.7%, 13% for platelet recipients, nearly 0 for red blood cells and plasma.
Oral transmission is reported with contaminated food or drinks, leading to outbreaks.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.title: Chagas Disease Prevalence and Risk Factors
items.content: The prevalence of Chagas disease is highest in Bolivia (6.1%), Argentina (3.6%), and Paraguay (2.1%). Urbanization and migration from rural areas have increased transmission risks in cities.
keyPoints: Highest prevalence: Bolivia (6.1%), Argentina (3.6%), Paraguay (2.1%)
Urban settings risk factor: recent history of migration from rural areas
Prevalence may exceed 40% in highly endemic regions
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Global Spread and Challenges in Chagas Disease Control
items.content: Chagas disease has spread to nonendemic countries due to global travel, with cases reported in North America, Western Europe, Australia, and Japan. Despite public health efforts, challenges like vector adaptation and low awareness persist.
keyPoints: Nonendemic country cases: US (up to 300,000), Western Europe (68,000–123,000)
Challenges: vector adaptation, resurgence in sprayed areas, resistance to insecticides
Low awareness among healthcare professionals
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Pathogenesis and Immune Response in Chagas Disease
items.content: Chagas disease pathogenesis involves complex interactions between T. cruzi parasites and the host immune response. After initial infection, trypomastigotes enter the bloodstream and differentiate into amastigotes within nucleated cells. The innate immune system triggers a T helper 1 response, reducing parasitemia to subpatent levels within 4–8 weeks, marking the end of the acute phase.
keyPoints: Trypomastigotes enter the bloodstream and differentiate into amastigotes in nucleated cells.
Innate immune response leads to a T helper 1 response with proinflammatory cytokines production.
Parasitemia is reduced to subpatent levels within 4–8 weeks, ending the acute phase.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Factors Influencing Cardiomyopathy and Reactivation in Chagas Disease
items.content: Cardiomyopathy in Chagas disease may result from persistent parasite proliferation and an inability to downregulate the immune response, causing cell-mediated damage. Genetic variation also contributes to cardiomyopathy development. Factors like HIV infection or immunosuppressive therapies can increase amastigote replication, leading to reactivation, especially when CD4+ T cell count falls below 100/μL.
keyPoints: Persistent parasite proliferation and immune response imbalance contribute to cardiomyopathy.
Genetic variation may influence cardiomyopathy development.
Reactivation risk is ~20% in HIV-positive patients without antiretroviral therapy, particularly when CD4+ T cell count <100/μL.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Understanding the Chronic Phase Progression in Chagas Disease
items.content: Chagas disease, caused by T. cruzi infection, progresses through an acute phase and a chronic phase. While most individuals remain asymptomatic during the chronic phase, 30-40% develop organ damage over decades. Cardiac complications are common, with bundle branch blocks often being early signs.
keyPoints: 60-70% of infected individuals never develop apparent tissue damage (indeterminate form).
30-40% progress to detectable organ damages (determinate form) over decades.
Chronic complications include cardiac (20-30%), digestive (5-20%), or mixed (5-10%) disorders.
Bundle branch blocks are usually the first signs in patients with cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.title: Importance of Screening for Chagas Disease
items.content: Chagas disease is often undiagnosed until chronic complications arise. Screening is crucial, especially in non-endemic areas, when symptoms or risk factors are present. This includes individuals with Latin American origins, history of unscreened blood transfusions, or potential transmission risks.
keyPoints: More than 90% of infections go undiagnosed until chronic complications develop.
Screening is recommended for Latin Americans with evocative symptoms and signs, including ECG abnormalities.
Risk factors include Chagas disease in family members, origins from endemic areas, or history of unscreened blood transfusions.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.title: Diagnosis of Chagas Disease in Acute and Chronic Phases
items.content: During the acute phase of Chagas disease, diagnosis is best achieved through detection of circulating parasites via microscopy after concentration or by PCR due to high parasitemia. In the chronic phase, when parasitemia becomes undetectable by microscopy, serologic tests detecting anti-T. cruzi IgG are used. Diagnosis confirmation requires two positive serologic tests using different techniques and antigens.
keyPoints: Detection of circulating parasites by microscopy or PCR during acute phases
Diagnosis in chronic phase relies on immunologic tests for anti-T. cruzi IgG
Two positive serologic tests with different techniques confirm diagnosis in chronic phase
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.title: Disease Staging and Evaluation of Complications in Chagas Disease
items.content: After confirming T. cruzi infection, clinicians should evaluate for complications using a thorough cardiac, neurologic, and digestive history along with clinical examination. A 12-lead ECG is used to screen for cardiomyopathy, with abnormalities such as right bundle branch block or ventricular premature beats indicating the need for further investigation through echocardiography or Holter tests.
keyPoints: Initial evaluation includes cardiac, neurologic, and digestive history
12-lead ECG screens for Chagas-associated cardiomyopathy
Common ECG abnormalities: right bundle branch block, left anterior fascicular block, ventricular premature beats
Echocardiography and 24-h Holter test assess chamber dilation, apical aneurysm, ventricular dysfunction, arrhythmias
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.title: Optimal Timing and Efficacy of Chagas Disease Treatment
items.content: Treatment for $T. cruzi$ infection is most effective during the acute phase, including congenital cases, and the early chronic phase in patients under 18 years old, achieving a cure rate of 60–100%. For adults with indeterminate chronic phase or chronic cardiomyopathy, treatment efficacy is uncertain or nonexistent, respectively.
keyPoints: Benznidazole and nifurtimox are effective when administered for ≥30 days.
Cure rates during acute and early chronic phases in patients <18 years old range from 60–100%.
Treatment has no impact on disease progression or mortality risk in adults with chronic cardiomyopathy.
Decision to treat patients >18 years old with indeterminate form should be individualized.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.title: Considerations and Contraindications for Chagas Disease Treatment
items.content: Treatment is contraindicated during pregnancy, advanced renal or hepatic failure. Benznidazole is preferred over nifurtimox in adults due to better tolerance. Close monitoring is necessary, and treatment duration remains debated with interest in shorter courses.
keyPoints: Contraindications include pregnancy and advanced renal/hepatic failure.
Benznidazole is recommended first-line for adults due to better drug tolerance.
Close clinical and biologic monitoring required during treatment.
Optimal treatment duration is debated, with interest in shorter courses.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.title: Management Strategies for Chagas Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Chagas cardiomyopathy management involves heart failure, conduction disturbances, and ventricular arrhythmia guidelines. Early treatment with amiodarone or implantation of a cardioverter defibrillator is crucial due to the high risk of sudden death from pathologic electrophysiologic abnormalities.
keyPoints: High risk of sudden death necessitates early intervention with amiodarone or cardioverter defibrillator for pathologic electrophysiologic abnormalities.
Anticoagulation is recommended for primary and secondary prevention in the presence of intramural thrombus or apical aneurysm.
Chagas cardiomyopathy often leads to heart transplantation, which shows better results than other etiologies.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.title: Post-Treatment Monitoring and Follow-Up in Chagas Disease
items.content: Defining optimal cure post-treatment is challenging. Serologic follow-up is essential during the acute phase, while no assay reliably documents response in the chronic phase. Negative seroconversion timing varies: months to 2 years for acute-phase treatment, decades for childhood infections.
keyPoints: Serologic follow-up is crucial for posttreatment monitoring in the acute phase; chronic phase lacks a proven assay for response documentation.
Negative seroconversion occurs within months to 2 years if treated during the acute phase, but takes decades when infection begins in childhood.
A positive posttreatment PCR indicates treatment failure; negative PCR cannot be reliably interpreted due to low sensitivity in the chronic phase.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.title: Effective Strategies for Preventing Chagas Disease Transmission
items.content: In the absence of a vaccine, prevention focuses on primary measures to stop T. cruzi transmission, secondary measures to avoid complications, and tertiary measures to reduce morbidity and mortality. Key strategies include screening blood and organ donations in endemic areas and among high-risk immigrant populations, implementing vector control through insecticide-impregnated bed nets, and early identification and treatment of cases.
keyPoints: Screening of blood and organ donations is crucial in endemic areas and for high-risk immigrants.
Vector control using insecticide-impregnated bed nets is effective against reduviid bug bites.
Early screening and treatment reduce complications and secondary transmission, especially congenital transmission.
Identification and early treatment of cardiac complications improve disease outcomes.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Global Challenges in Addressing Chagas Disease
items.content: Chagas disease is a global health issue affecting vulnerable populations across four continents. Progress is hindered by limited research, financial and political commitment, problematic drug access, and lack of incentives for new drug development. Global phenomena such as climate change, population aging, rising noncommunicable diseases, and increased immunosuppressive drug use may alter the disease's epidemiology and burden.
keyPoints: Chagas disease affects vulnerable populations on four continents.
Lack of research, financial commitment, and political will hampers progress.
Drug production and access remain problematic in many countries, including the U.S.
Climatic changes, aging populations, noncommunicable diseases, and immunosuppressive drugs may impact disease epidemiology.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Epidemiologic Trends and Control Measures of Human African Trypanosomiasis (HAT)
items.content: Human African Trypanosomiasis, caused by T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense, has seen significant declines in reported cases due to systematic control measures.
keyPoints: T. b. gambiense HAT endemic in 24 countries of western and central Africa; reported cases fell by 98% from 1999 to 2020 (27,862 to 565).
T. b. rhodesiense HAT endemic in eastern and southeastern Africa; reported cases fell by 84% during the same period (619 to 98).
Most T. b. gambiense cases reported by DRC (70%) in 2020, while Malawi reported most T. b. rhodesiense cases (91%).
WHO targets for elimination: <2000 annual cases reached since 2018; 83% reduction in area at risk reporting ≥1 case/10,000 people/year by 2020.
Next WHO goal is global elimination of transmission by 2030.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission Dynamics and Risk Factors for Human African Trypanosomiasis (HAT)
items.content: Understanding the transmission dynamics and risk factors is crucial for preventing HAT, with distinct differences between T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense.
keyPoints: Humans are the predominant reservoir for T. b. gambiense; infection primarily via tsetse fly bites during activities near rivers.
T. b. rhodesiense causes zoonosis, with wild/domesticated animals as reservoirs; humans infected in woodland savannah.
High-risk groups for T. b. rhodesiense include honey gatherers, game park rangers, poachers, and firewood collectors.
Imported cases occur among African immigrants and travelers; long-term travelers (>30 days) at increased risk of T. b. gambiense HAT.
Most imported cases of T. b. rhodesiense HAT are short-term travelers visiting game parks.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanisms of Trypanosome Resistance in Human Serum
items.content: T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense resist lytic factors in human serum through specific proteins, allowing them to infect humans. The resistance is mediated by the serum resistance-associated protein in T. b. rhodesiense and the TgsGP gene in T. b. gambiense.
keyPoints: T. b. rhodesiense uses the serum resistance-associated protein for resistance.
T. b. gambiense employs mechanisms involving the TgsGP gene.
Human APOL1 variants, prevalent among individuals of African ancestry, offer protection against livestock trypanosome species but increase susceptibility to chronic kidney disease.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: CNS Invasion and Pathology in HAT
items.content: The CNS invasion occurs weeks to months after initial infection with T. b. rhodesiense, or months to years with T. b. gambiense, marking the second stage of Human African Trypanosomiasis (HAT). This stage is characterized by trypanosomes or mononuclear cells in the CSF and predominantly affects white matter.
keyPoints: CNS invasion occurs weeks to months for T. b. rhodesiense, months to years for T. b. gambiense.
Second-stage HAT defined by trypanosomes or mononuclear cells in CSF.
White matter is predominantly affected with perivascular proliferation of astrocytes, microglial cells, and Mott's cells containing IgM.
Cerebral cortex and neurons are spared until terminal stages; neuropsychiatric symptoms can resolve partially or completely after treatment.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Why Early Diagnosis of HAT is Crucial
items.content: Early diagnosis of Human African Trypanosomiasis (HAT) is essential as it is usually lethal without treatment. Physicians should consider HAT in the differential diagnosis for patients with a history of travel or residence in at-risk sub-Saharan African countries.
keyPoints: HAT is often fatal if untreated, emphasizing the need for prompt diagnosis.

Consider HAT when evaluating persistent and intermittent fever, headaches, progressive neuropsychiatric disorders, and systemic inflammation signs, particularly in patients with a history of travel to or residence in sub-Saharan Africa.
For T. b. gambiense, suspect HAT even if exposure was several years ago.
For T. b. rhodesiense, consider HAT in cases of acute febrile illness with recent tsetse fly exposure in eastern Africa, especially when malaria tests are negative.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.title: How to Suspect and Diagnose Different Forms of HAT
items.content: Differentiating between T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense forms of HAT is crucial for diagnosis, based on clinical presentation and travel history.
keyPoints: T. b. gambiense should be suspected in patients with persistent fevers, headaches, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and systemic inflammation signs, even if exposure was years ago.
T. b. rhodesiense is more likely in patients with acute febrile illness following recent tsetse fly exposure in eastern Africa.
Negative malaria tests should prompt consideration of HAT due to T. b. rhodesiense.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.title: Differences in Clinical Presentation Between T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense
items.content: T. b. gambiense HAT typically presents as a slowly evolving illness with a long incubation period, while T. b. rhodesiense HAT is an acute febrile illness with a short incubation period. However, exceptions exist such as acute forms of T. b. gambiense and chronic forms of T. b. rhodesiense in certain geographic areas.
keyPoints: T. b. gambiense: long incubation (months to years), prolonged disease course
T. b. rhodesiense: short incubation (<3 weeks), shorter disease course
Exceptions: acute T. b. gambiense, chronic T. b. rhodesiense in southern range
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Systemic Features of HAT and Diagnostic Considerations
items.content: Patients with HAT may present with irregular fever, fatigue, malaise, myalgia, rashes, pruritus, lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and signs of myocarditis or pericarditis. Lymph node enlargement is a classic sign, detected in 38-85% of patients.
keyPoints: Irregular fever, fatigue, malaise, myalgia
Rashes (trypanids) more common in Caucasians
Lymphadenopathy: 38-85% detection rate
Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly possible
Myocarditis/pericarditis signs may be silent
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Neuropsychiatric Features of Second-Stage HAT
items.content: Second-stage HAT can lead to neuropsychiatric symptoms such as sleep disturbances, mood changes, and motor or mental disorders. These features become more severe in the second stage and can mimic psychiatric illnesses.
keyPoints: Sleep disturbances: daytime somnolence, nocturnal insomnia
Mood/behavioral changes: irritability, indifference, depression, psychosis
Neurologic syndromes: pyramidal, extrapyramidal, cerebellar-related
Final stage: decreased consciousness, dementia, epilepsy
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Systemic Features of Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis
items.content: Patients with both first- and second-stage Chagas disease or African trypanosomiasis may present with systemic features such as high fever, headaches, diffuse myalgia, arthralgia, pruritus, and edema of the face and legs. Lymphadenopathies are common in submandibular, axillary, and inguinal regions. Myocarditis and pericarditis can influence clinical outcomes despite not being prominent findings in large case series. Conduction abnormalities like atrioventricular block may occur in travelers. Sepsis-like features with disseminated intravascular coagulation and multiple-organ failure are possible in terminal stages.
keyPoints: Fever, headaches, diffuse myalgia, arthralgia
Pruritus, edema of face and legs
Lymphadenopathies in submandibular, axillary, inguinal regions
Mild to moderate hepatomegaly and splenomegaly in minority of patients
Myocarditis and pericarditis influence clinical course
Conduction abnormalities (atrioventricular block) in travelers
Sepsis-like features with disseminated intravascular coagulation, multiple-organ failure in terminal stage
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Neuropsychiatric Features of African Trypanosomiasis
items.content: Neuropsychiatric symptoms and signs are common in both T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense HAT, with a notable difference being the rapid progression to coma and death in T. b. rhodesiense disease.
keyPoints: Neuropsychiatric symptoms similar in T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense HAT
Rapid evolution toward coma and death in T. b. rhodesiense
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Confirmation of T. b. gambiense HAT
items.content: For T. b. gambiense HAT, diagnostic confirmation requires direct observation of trypanosomes in body fluids or tissues due to non-specific clinical features.
keyPoints: Clinical features like anemia and thrombocytopenia are not specific enough for treatment initiation without confirmation.
Direct microscopic observation of motile trypanosomes in lymph, blood, skin, or CSF confirms diagnosis.
Sensitivity of direct observation on cervical lymph node puncture is 50–65%.
Microhematocrit centrifugation technique improves sensitivity to 40–60%.
Miniature anion-exchange centrifugation technique has ~90% sensitivity.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Screening for T. b. gambiense HAT
items.content: Serologic methods, particularly the card agglutination test for trypanosomiasis (CATT), are preferred for initial screening of T. b. gambiense HAT.
keyPoints: CATT is widely used in endemic areas with sensitivity 69–100% and specificity 84–99%.
Lateral flow tests based on recombinant antigens have comparable performance to CATT.
Other serologic tests like ELISA are available but less commonly used.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Staging of HAT in T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense
items.content: Staging is crucial for treatment decisions, particularly for T. b. rhodesiense due to stage-dependent treatments.
keyPoints: Second-stage HAT is defined by CSF leukocyte count >5/μL or presence of trypanosomes.
Staging is not obligatory when using fexinidazole for both stages, except in young children and severe cases.
For T. b. rhodesiense, staging remains obligatory due to stage-dependent treatment modalities.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.title: Fexinidazole Use and Efficacy in HAT Treatment
items.content: Fexinidazole is an effective oral treatment for first-stage and nonsevere second-stage Human African Trypanosomiasis (HAT) caused by T. b. gambiense, with a cure rate greater than 95%. It is administered over 10 days in two phases: a 4-day loading phase followed by a 6-day maintenance phase. However, its efficacy decreases to an 87% cure rate for severe second-stage HAT (≥100 leukocytes/μL in the CSF).
keyPoints: >95% cure rate for first-stage and nonsevere second-stage HAT (<100 leukocytes/μL in the CSF)
10-day treatment: 4-day loading phase, 6-day maintenance phase
<87% cure rate for severe second-stage HAT (≥100 leukocytes/μL in the CSF)
Adverse reactions include vomiting, headache, and neuropsychiatric disorders
Contraindicated in hepatic insufficiency or increased risk of QT interval prolongation
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.title: Nifurtimox-Eflornithine Combination Therapy for Severe HAT
items.content: The nifurtimox-eflornithine combination therapy is highly effective and safe for treating second-stage HAT, including severe cases (≥100 leukocytes/μL in the CSF), with a cure rate greater than 95%. Common adverse reactions include gastrointestinal disturbances, headache, anorexia, and reversible bone marrow toxicity. Convulsions and psychosis are rare (<5% of patients).
keyPoints: >95% cure rate for second-stage HAT, including severe cases (≥100 leukocytes/μL in the CSF)
Common adverse reactions: gastrointestinal disturbances, headache, anorexia, reversible bone marrow toxicity
<5% incidence of convulsions and psychosis
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.title: Melarsoprol Treatment and Risks in Second-Stage T. b. Rhodesiense HAT
items.content: Melarsoprol is used for second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT due to the ineffectiveness of eflornithine against this stage. It carries significant risks, including reactive encephalopathy with a mortality rate ranging from 10–70%. The risk of death during or after treatment is between 5–10%.
keyPoints: Melarsoprol remains the treatment for second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT.
Reactive encephalopathy occurs in 5–18% of patients with a mortality rate of 10–70%.
>5-10% risk of dying during or after melarsoprol treatment.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Global Elimination Goals for Sleeping Sickness by 2030
items.content: The elimination of sleeping sickness as a public health problem has been achieved through increased control activities, improved funding, and the end of civil conflicts. Continued efforts in research, development, and implementation of diagnostic, therapeutic, and vector control tools are crucial to sustain progress and aim for global transmission elimination by 2030.
keyPoints: Elimination as a public health problem achieved due to increased control activities and improved funding.
Goal: Global elimination of transmission by 2030.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mode of transmission for T. cruzi?
options: Congenital transmission from mother to newborn
items.explanation: T. cruzi is not transmitted through direct person-to-person contact.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.question: What percentage of newborns from infected mothers are at risk for congenital T. cruzi infection?
items.explanation: Congenital transmission of T. cruzi occurs in 1–10% of newborns from infected mothers.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.question: Which form of T. cruzi enters the human body through cutaneous breaks, mucosae, or conjunctivae?
items.explanation: The infective metacyclic trypomastigote form of T. cruzi enters the human body through cutaneous breaks, mucosae, or conjunctivae.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.question: Which country has the highest reported prevalence of T. cruzi infection?
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence is highest in Bolivia at 6.1%.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What has contributed to the decline in the prevalence and incidence of Chagas disease in recent decades?
options: Improved housing and public health interventions
items.explanation: The passage mentions improved housing, socioeconomic conditions, and public health interventions as reasons for the decline.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is a major risk factor for T. cruzi transmission in urban settings?
items.explanation: The passage identifies a recent history of migration from rural areas as the main risk factor in urban settings.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which immune response is predominantly triggered by the innate immune system in response to parasite mucins and DNA during Chagas disease?
items.explanation: The innate immune response to parasite mucins and DNA leads to a predominantly T helper 1 response.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.question: What is the clinical significance of autonomic cell destruction in Chagas disease?
options: Its exact clinical significance remains to be clarified.
It leads directly to megaviscera syndrome.
It results in a decrease in parasitemia.
items.explanation: The exact clinical significance of autonomic cell destruction due to vagal and sympathetic denervation is yet to be clarified.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which factors may increase the risk of T. cruzi reactivation in HIV-positive patients?
options: High CD4+ T cell count with antiretroviral therapy
Low CD4+ T cell count without antiretroviral therapies
Use of antibiotics targeting intracellular parasites
items.explanation: In HIV-positive patients, the risk of reactivation is increased when there is a low CD4+ T cell count without antiretroviral therapies.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which phase of T. cruzi infection is usually undetected and undiagnosed in most individuals?
items.explanation: The acute phase remains undetected and undiagnosed in most individuals.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.question: What percentage of T. cruzi-infected individuals progress to detectable organ damage during the chronic phase?
items.explanation: The remaining 30–40% of individuals progress toward detectable organ damages during the chronic phase.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.question: What is usually the first sign in patients with cardiomyopathy due to T. cruzi infection?
items.explanation: In patients with cardiomyopathy, bundle branch blocks are usually the first signs.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.question: Which diagnostic method is most appropriate during the acute phase of Chagas disease when parasitemia levels are high?
options: Immunologic tests detecting anti-T. cruzi IgG
Microscopy after concentration or nucleic acid-based assay (PCR)
Serologic tests using different techniques and targeting different antigens
items.explanation: During the acute phase with high parasitemia, detection of circulating parasites by microscopy after concentration or PCR is most appropriate.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: What is recommended if serologic tests for Chagas disease during the chronic phase yield discordant results?
options: Perform a third serologic test using a different technique and antigen
Repeat the initial two serologic tests
Use rapid diagnostic tests as confirmation
items.explanation: A third serologic test is warranted if there are discordant results from the initial two serologic tests.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: What diagnostic procedure should be performed after 4 weeks of age to accurately diagnose congenital Chagas infection?
options: Microscopy or PCR on cord and/or peripheral blood
PCR, as it is more accurate later in life
items.explanation: A conventional serologic assay should be performed after 4 weeks of age for an accurate diagnosis.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: Which drug is recommended as the first-line treatment for adults with Chagas disease?
options: Neither, a different drug should be used
items.explanation: Benznidazole is better tolerated than nifurtimox in adults and is recommended as the first-line treatment.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.question: During which phase of Chagas disease is treatment most effective?
items.explanation: Treatment is most effective during the acute (including congenital) phase and the early chronic phase, especially in patients <18 years of age.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.question: For which condition are benznidazole and nifurtimox not effective?
items.explanation: Neither benznidazole nor nifurtimox is effective against digestive complications.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.question: Which treatment is recommended for primary and secondary prevention of cardioembolic events in Chagas cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Anticoagulation is recommended for the prevention of cardioembolic events in the presence of an intramural thrombus or apical aneurysm.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.question: What intervention may improve upper gastrointestinal symptoms in the early stage of digestive dysmotility due to Chagas disease?
items.explanation: Pneumatic balloon dilatation, along with drugs releasing the lower esophageal sphincter and laparoscopic myotomy, can improve upper gastrointestinal symptoms in early-stage digestive dysmotility.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.question: What is a key component of the basic yearly assessment for patients with Chagas disease?
items.explanation: The basic yearly assessment includes history-taking, clinical examination, and a 12-lead ECG.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.question: Which strategy is considered effective and cost-effective for curbing intradomiciliary transmission of Chagas disease?
items.explanation: Sustained vector control is effective and cost-effective for reducing intradomiciliary transmission.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What has been highly cost-effective in Spain regarding Chagas disease prevention?
options: Screening of blood donations from Latin American migrants
Use of insecticide-impregnated bed nets for all residents
Screening of child-bearing-age and pregnant Latin American migrant women
items.explanation: Screening of child-bearing-age and pregnant Latin American migrant women has been highly cost-effective in Spain.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What is a major barrier to progress against Chagas disease as mentioned in the passage?
options: High prevalence of infection in high-income countries
Lack of research and development, and financial/political commitment
items.explanation: Progress against Chagas disease is limited by a lack of research and development and financial/political commitment.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which two subspecies of Trypanosoma brucei affect humans?
options: T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense
T. b. evansi and T. b. equiperdum
items.explanation: The passage specifies that T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense are the two pathogenic subspecies affecting humans.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What was the target number of annual HAT cases set by WHO to consider it a public health problem?
items.explanation: The passage states that WHO's primary indicator for elimination as a public health problem was the number of cases reported annually, with a target of <2000.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which region are humans predominantly infected by T. b. gambiense?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that almost all patients with T. b. gambiense are infected by tsetse flies along or near rivers.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which protein is responsible for serum resistance in T. b. rhodesiense?
items.explanation: The serum resistance-associated protein is responsible for resistance in T. b. rhodesiense.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What causes the cyclic variation in trypanosome density in the blood?
options: Host's immune response decimating parasite population until new variants emerge
items.explanation: The cyclic variation is due to the host's immune response decimating the parasite population until offspring with different variant surface glycoproteins emerge.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which tissues are commonly involved in T. b. rhodesiense infection during the first stage of illness?
options: Skin, skeletal muscles, serous membranes, and heart
items.explanation: Trypanosomes multiply in the skin, skeletal muscles, serous membranes, and heart during the first stage of illness.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which subspecies of Trypanosoma brucei is typically associated with a chronic course and should be suspected in patients presenting with progressive neuropsychiatric disorders?
items.explanation: HAT due to T. b. gambiense is associated with a chronic course and progressive neuropsychiatric disorders.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.question: In which region should HAT be suspected in patients presenting with an acute febrile illness and recent exposure to tsetse flies?
items.explanation: HAT due to T. b. rhodesiense should be suspected in patients with acute febrile illness and recent exposure to tsetse flies in Eastern Africa.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.question: What is a crucial step for diagnosing HAT in patients who have traveled or lived in at-risk sub-Saharan African countries?
options: Obtaining a thorough recent and remote travel history
items.explanation: A thorough recent and remote travel history is crucial for diagnosing HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.question: Which Trypanosoma brucei subspecies typically presents with a slowly evolving illness and a long incubation period?
options: Both T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense
Neither T. b. rhodesiense nor T. b. gambiense
items.explanation: T. b. gambiense HAT is a slowly evolving illness with a long incubation period.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical duration of the incubation period for T. b. rhodesiense HAT?
items.explanation: T. b. rhodesiense HAT has a short (<3-week) incubation period.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which symptom is more common among travelers with T. b. gambiense HAT compared to natives?
items.explanation: Fever is more frequent among travelers than among natives with T. b. gambiense HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In which stage of T. b. gambiense HAT are symptoms such as decreased libido and amenorrhea most likely to occur?
items.explanation: Loss of libido, impotence, and amenorrhea are common in second-stage patients.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is commonly associated with both first- and second-stage Chagas disease?
items.explanation: Fever can be high in both stages of Chagas disease, often with headaches, myalgia, and arthralgia.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which organ enlargement is documented in a minority of patients with Chagas disease?
items.explanation: Mild to moderate hepatomegaly and splenomegaly are documented in a minority of patients.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is the notable difference in neuropsychiatric features between T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense HAT?
options: T. b. rhodesiense evolves more rapidly toward coma and death
T. b. gambiense presents with sepsis-like features
Both have similar progression rates to coma
Neuropsychiatric symptoms are absent in T. b. rhodesiense
items.explanation: The notable exception in T. b. rhodesiense disease is a more rapid evolution toward coma and death.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which test is preferred for screening T. b. gambiense HAT in endemic areas?
options: The card agglutination test for trypanosomiasis (CATT)
Microscopic observation of trypanosomes in blood smears
items.explanation: The CATT is the preferred screening tool due to its sensitivity and specificity.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most sensitive method for diagnostic confirmation of T. b. gambiense HAT?
options: Direct observation on a wet preparation of lymph
Microscopic examination of stained blood smears
items.explanation: The miniature anion-exchange centrifugation technique has the highest sensitivity (~90%).
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: For which stage of HAT is lumbar puncture used to examine CSF?
options: Neither, as it's not required for staging
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture is used to examine CSF for staging second-stage HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method has not been fully validated for T. b. gambiense HAT?
options: Microscopic observation of trypanosomes in lymph
Molecular methods based on PCR or loop-mediated isothermal amplification
items.explanation: Molecular methods have not been fully validated for diagnostic purposes.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for using fexinidazole in the treatment of HAT?
items.explanation: Fexinidazole is contraindicated in patients with hepatic insufficiency or at increased risk of QT interval prolongation.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.question: What is the recommended administration schedule for fexinidazole when treating HAT?
options: 10 days divided into a 5-day loading phase and a 5-day maintenance phase
7 days with no specific division between phases
10 days divided into a 4-day loading phase and a 6-day maintenance phase
items.explanation: Fexinidazole is administered for 10 days, divided into a 4-day loading phase and a 6-day maintenance phase.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.question: Which treatment has been shown to have an efficacy of >95% in patients with second-stage HAT, including those with severe illness?
items.explanation: Nifurtimox-eflornithine combination therapy is very effective (>95% cure rate) and safe in patients with second-stage HAT, including severe illness.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which drug is used as first-line treatment for first-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT?
items.explanation: Suramin has been used for over 90 years and remains the first-line treatment for first-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is a life-threatening adverse event associated with melarsoprol in the treatment of second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT?
items.explanation: Reactive encephalopathy is a life-threatening adverse event that occurs in 5–18% of patients treated with melarsoprol for second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is the efficacy rate of fexinidazole in treating first and second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT as reported in studies conducted in Malawi and Uganda?
items.explanation: Fexinidazole was administered to 45 patients with first-stage (n = 10) and second-stage (n = 35) T. b. rhodesiense HAT, curing 43 (96%).
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.statement: T. cruzi can be transmitted by bedbugs.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that bedbugs actually transmit T. cruzi to humans.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.statement: Oral transmission of T. cruzi can occur after ingestion of contaminated food or drinks.
items.explanation: Oral transmission is increasingly reported after ingestion of contaminated food (berries) or drinks (fruit or sugar cane juice).
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.statement: Chagas disease is no longer found in any endemic countries.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that Chagas disease remains prevalent in certain regions, despite progress.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: International tourists visiting endemic countries are at high risk of contracting Chagas disease.
items.explanation: The passage states that international tourists have a very low risk of infection.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Genome-wide association studies suggest that genetic variation does not contribute to the development of cardiomyopathy in Chagas disease.
items.explanation: Genetic variation is suggested to contribute to the development of cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.statement: The rarity of digestive tract involvement north of the Amazon basin suggests no influence of specific parasitic and host genetic factors on disease course.
items.explanation: Specific parasitic and host genetic factors may influence the disease course, as suggested by the rarity of digestive tract involvement north of the Amazon basin.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Most individuals infected with T. cruzi develop apparent tissue damage during the chronic phase.
items.explanation: 60–70% of these individuals never develop apparent tissue damage, known as the indeterminate form.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.statement: Advanced cardiac damage in T. cruzi infection has a worse prognosis than ischemic heart disease.
items.explanation: Advanced cardiac damage from T. cruzi entails a worse prognosis than other cardiomyopathies, notably ischemic heart disease.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | Chagas Disease (American Trypanosomiasis)

items.statement: PCR is the preferred method for primary diagnosis during the chronic phase of Chagas disease.
items.explanation: During the chronic phase, PCR has limited sensitivity and is not preferred for primary diagnosis.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.statement: An abnormal ECG result or suggestive cardiac symptoms in Chagas disease should prompt further investigation.
items.explanation: Abnormal ECG results or suggestive cardiac symptoms warrant additional diagnostic evaluations.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.statement: Treatment with benznidazole or nifurtimox has been proven to be safe during pregnancy.
items.explanation: A negative pregnancy test is mandatory before initiating treatment as these drugs have not been proven to be safe in pregnancy.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.statement: Benznidazole has an impact on disease progression and mortality risk in adults with chronic cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: In adults with chronic cardiomyopathy, benznidazole has no impact on disease progression and mortality risk.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.statement: A positive result in posttreatment PCR indicates treatment failure.
items.explanation: A positive result in a posttreatment PCR indeed indicates treatment failure.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.statement: Negative seroconversion after treatment is always achieved within months, regardless of the infection phase.
items.explanation: The time needed for negative seroconversion depends on the duration of infection and can take decades in adults infected during childhood.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · NONETIOLOGIC TREATMENT | CLINICAL FOLLOW-UP

items.statement: The production and registration of existing drugs for Chagas disease are not problematic in many countries, including the United States.
items.explanation: Production and registration of existing drugs remain problematic in many countries, including the United States.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Climatic changes are unlikely to impact the epidemiology and burden of Chagas disease.
items.explanation: Climatic changes are likely to impact the epidemiology, clinical course, and burden of Chagas disease.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PREVENTION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: T. b. rhodesiense primarily infects humans through contact with domesticated animals.
items.explanation: The passage states that T. b. rhodesiense causes zoonosis in wild and domesticated animals, but humans are infected via tsetse bites.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Most cases of T. b. gambiense HAT were reported by the Democratic Republic of the Congo in 2020.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that most cases of T. b. gambiense HAT in 2020 were reported by the DRC.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense can infect humans because they resist lytic factors in human serum.
items.explanation: Both species resist lytic factors such as APOL1, allowing them to infect humans.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: The cerebral cortex and neurons are affected early in the second stage of HAT.
items.explanation: The cerebral cortex and neurons are spared until the terminal stages of illness.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: HAT due to T. b. gambiense can present with symptoms even if the last exposure occurred several years previously.
items.explanation: T. b. gambiense HAT can have a long incubation period, presenting symptoms years after exposure.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.statement: HAT is typically self-limiting and does not require treatment.
items.explanation: HAT is usually lethal in the absence of treatment, so it requires prompt diagnosis and management.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.statement: Trypanosomal chancre is more commonly reported among natives in endemic areas than travelers.
items.explanation: The occurrence of trypanosomal chancre is reported more frequently among travelers.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: HIV co-infection increases the risk of HAT.
items.explanation: Concomitant HIV co-infection does not seem to predispose individuals to an increased risk of HAT.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Cardiac failure or arrhythmia are prominent findings in large case series of Chagas disease.
items.explanation: Clinical features of cardiac failure or arrhythmia have not been prominent findings in large case series.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Disseminated intravascular coagulation and multiple-organ failure can occur in the terminal stage of Chagas disease.
items.explanation: Sepsis-like features, with disseminated intravascular coagulation and multiple-organ failure, can occur in the terminal stage.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: The sensitivity of the CATT on undiluted blood or serum is consistently above 90% in all studies.
items.explanation: While the sensitivity is often >90%, it varies regionally and ranges from 69–100%.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Staging using CSF examination is obligatory for all T. b. rhodesiense HAT patients.
items.explanation: Staging remains an obligatory step due to stage-dependent treatment modalities.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.statement: Acoziborole is currently the preferred treatment for T. b. gambiense HAT.
items.explanation: Acoziborole could become the preferred treatment in the future, but its efficacy and safety need confirmation in a larger number of individuals.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE
items.statement: Pentamidine isethionate can be administered in peripheral health care centers in endemic countries.
items.explanation: Pentamidine is generally well tolerated and can therefore be administered in peripheral health care centers.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis | T. B. GAMBIENSE

items.subtopic: T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Eflornithine is effective against T. b. rhodesiense.
items.explanation: Eflornithine is ineffective against T. b. rhodesiense, and melarsoprol remains in use for second-stage treatment.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Relapses of T. b. rhodesiense HAT are very common after treatment.
items.explanation: Patients with second-stage T. b. rhodesiense HAT have a low risk of relapse, which is very rare.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · T. B. RHODESIENSE | PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION
items.assertion: Most human infections with T. cruzi in the Americas are intradomiciliary.
items.reason: Intradomiciliary transmission arises from a triatomine bite during nighttime sleep.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why most human infections with T. cruzi in the Americas are intradomiciliary.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis | DEFINITION | TRANSMISSION

items.assertion: Chagas disease distribution has expanded to nonendemic countries.
items.reason: Increased global travel and migration from endemic regions contribute to this expansion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Treatment should be undertaken for all children and women of child-bearing age.
items.reason: The decision to treat patients >18 years old with the indeterminate form of chronic phase is made on an individual basis after discussing pros and cons.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why treatment should be undertaken for all children and women of child-bearing age.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · ETIOLOGIC TREATMENT

items.reason: Humans are infected by T. b. rhodesiense via tsetse bites in woodland savannah.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why T. b. rhodesiense causes zoonosis; it explains how humans get infected.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Early diagnosis of HAT is crucial for patient survival.
items.reason: HAT can be lethal if left untreated, making early intervention essential.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early diagnosis is crucial.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · Human African Trypanosomiasis

items.assertion: Trypano-tolerance involves the long-term persistence of parasites without clinical features.
items.reason: Trypano-resistance is characterized by self-resolving first-stage infections.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and trypano-tolerance is correctly explained as involving parasite persistence without disease.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Trypanids are easily detectable on dark skin.
items.reason: Trypanids are almost impossible to detect on dark skin and have been reported only in Caucasians.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; trypanids are difficult to detect on dark skin.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Conduction abnormalities are reported in travelers with Chagas disease.
items.reason: Atrioventricular block has been observed in travelers, indicating conduction abnormalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Direct observation of trypanosomes on a wet preparation of lymph is the most sensitive diagnostic confirmation method.
items.reason: This method has limited sensitivity (50–65% in most studies).
items.explanation: The assertion is false because this method has limited sensitivity; the reason correctly states its low sensitivity.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Molecular methods applied to CSF are more accurate than classic methods for staging.
items.reason: Molecular methods have yielded false-positive results in a substantial proportion of cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as molecular methods are not more accurate; the reason correctly states their limitations.
items.reference: Chagas Disease and African Trypanosomiasis · DIAGNOSIS
